June 25, 2026

Interior Plaster vs Drywall: Which Is Right for Your Austin Home?

If you are building a new home in Austin or renovating a historic property in Hyde Park or Travis Heights, you will eventually face this question: plaster or drywall? Both create finished interior walls, but they are fundamentally different materials that deliver different results in durability, sound, feel, and cost. The right choice depends on your priorities, your budget, and the character you want your home to have.

This guide compares the two honestly so you can make a confident decision.

What Is Interior Plaster?

Interior plaster is a wet-applied wall finish made from gypsum, lime, or cement mixed with sand and water. It is troweled onto a substrate — traditionally wood lath, and in modern applications metal lath or a specialized plaster base board — in two or three coats that cure to form a hard, dense, seamless surface. Plaster has been the standard interior wall finish for centuries. Most Austin homes built before the 1950s have original plaster walls, and many owners of newer homes are rediscovering its qualities in renovations and custom builds.

What Is Drywall?

Drywall (also called sheetrock or gypsum board) is a factory-made panel of gypsum plaster pressed between two sheets of paper. The panels are screwed to wall framing, and the seams are covered with joint tape and compound, then sanded smooth. Drywall became dominant in the post-World War II building boom because it is faster and cheaper to install than traditional plaster, and it remains the standard wall finish in the vast majority of new Austin construction.

Side-by-Side Comparison

FeatureInterior PlasterDrywall
Surface hardnessVery hard and denseSofter, dents more easily
DurabilityExtremely durable, lasts 100+ yearsDurable but more prone to damage
Sound insulationSuperior — denser mass blocks more noiseStandard
Thermal massHigher — absorbs and releases heat slowlyLower
AestheticsSeamless, hand-finished characterSmooth and uniform
Texture optionsUnlimited — Venetian, sand, skip-trowel, smoothLimited to tape-and-texture methods
Crack resistanceCan develop hairline cracks over timeCracks at seams and corners
RepairabilityRequires skilled patchingEasier to patch for most homeowners
Installation speedSlower (multiple coats, cure time)Faster (hang, tape, finish)
CostHigherLower

Durability

This is where plaster truly shines. A plaster wall is rock-hard — try pushing a thumbtack into one and you will understand the difference immediately. That density makes plaster walls far more resistant to dents, dings, and everyday wear than drywall. In older Austin homes in neighborhoods like Clarksville, Old West Austin, and Pemberton Heights, original plaster walls from the 1920s and 1930s are still in excellent condition nearly a century later.

Drywall is serviceable and plenty tough for everyday life, but it dents more easily, is more susceptible to water damage, and shows wear sooner in high-traffic areas. In a home with active kids or pets, the difference becomes noticeable over the years.

Sound Insulation

If noise control matters to you, plaster is the clear winner. The greater mass and density of a plaster wall blocks significantly more sound transmission between rooms than a standard drywall partition. This is relevant in Austin homes where open floor plans are common and sound carries, or in media rooms, home offices, and bedrooms where quiet matters. Achieving comparable sound performance with drywall typically requires adding mass-loaded vinyl, extra drywall layers, or resilient channel — all of which add cost and complexity.

Aesthetics and Texture

Plaster offers a depth and character that drywall cannot replicate. A skilled plasterer can create finishes ranging from glass-smooth Venetian plaster to subtle hand-troweled textures that catch light and add warmth to a room. The seamless, jointless surface has an organic quality that feels distinctly different from flat drywall — and it is a major reason plaster is making a comeback in high-end Austin renovations and custom homes in areas like Westlake, Rollingwood, and the Lake Austin corridor.

Drywall finishes are more limited. Standard options include smooth (level 5), orange peel, knockdown, and skip-trowel, all applied over taped joints. These finishes are perfectly attractive, but they lack the hand-crafted depth of true plaster work.

Austin Climate Considerations

Austin's climate plays into this decision in a few ways:

  • **Thermal mass** — Plaster's higher density helps moderate indoor temperature swings, absorbing heat during the day and releasing it at night. In a home where cooling costs dominate the utility bill eight months of the year, that thermal buffer provides a subtle but real benefit.
  • **Humidity resistance** — Lime-based plasters are naturally resistant to mold and mildew, an advantage during Austin's humid spring months. Drywall's paper facing is more susceptible to mold when exposed to persistent moisture, which is why green board or paperless gypsum board is used in wet areas.
  • **Foundation movement** — Austin's clay soils cause foundation movement that can stress interior walls. Both plaster and drywall can crack from movement, but drywall typically cracks at seams, while plaster may develop hairline cracks across the surface. Neither is immune; the difference is how the damage presents.

Cost Comparison

Interior plaster is more expensive than drywall — there is no way around that. For a typical Austin home, expect plaster to cost roughly two to three times what drywall would for the same wall area. The premium comes from the labor: plastering is a multi-coat, hand-applied craft that requires experienced tradespeople and multiple visits as each coat cures.

For a new 2,000 square foot home in Austin, drywall installation (hung, taped, textured, and primed) might run $8,000 to $15,000. Full plaster on the same home could run $20,000 to $40,000 or more depending on the finish complexity. Venetian plaster and other decorative finishes push the upper end higher.

Many homeowners find a middle ground: drywall for most of the home and plaster as an accent in high-visibility areas like the living room, entryway, fireplace wall, or primary bedroom. This approach delivers the character of plaster where it matters most while keeping the overall budget manageable.

Maintenance and Repair

Drywall is easier for a homeowner to patch. Small holes and dents can be filled with spackle and sanded smooth in an afternoon. Plaster repair is more involved — matching the existing texture and hardness requires skill, and patches can telegraph if the technique or material is not right. For older Austin homes with extensive plaster, working with a professional who understands the material is the best way to maintain the walls' integrity and appearance.

Which Should You Choose?

The decision comes down to priorities:

  • **Choose plaster** if you value durability, sound insulation, distinctive aesthetics, and are building or renovating for the long term. Plaster is an investment that pays off over decades.
  • **Choose drywall** if budget, speed, and ease of future repairs are your primary concerns. Drywall is a proven, practical material that serves the vast majority of Austin homes well.
  • **Consider a hybrid approach** if you want the character of plaster in key rooms without the cost of plastering the entire home.

Get Expert Advice

Star Stucco of Austin offers interior plaster services for homeowners across the Austin metro who want the look and performance of real plaster. Whether you are restoring an original plaster home in Old Enfield or adding a Venetian plaster accent wall in a new build in Dripping Springs, our team can help. Frequently Asked Questions

Is interior plaster worth the extra cost?

For many homeowners, yes. Plaster walls are significantly harder, more sound-resistant, and more aesthetically distinctive than drywall, and they can last a century or more. If you are building a forever home or renovating a high-end property, the investment often pays for itself in durability and resale value. A hybrid approach — plaster in key rooms, drywall elsewhere — offers a practical middle ground.

Can you plaster over existing drywall?

Yes. A skim coat of plaster can be applied over sound, properly prepared drywall to give it the look and feel of a plaster surface. This is a popular option for Austin homeowners who want the plaster aesthetic without the cost of tearing out existing drywall. The result is not as thick or dense as full traditional plaster but offers a noticeable upgrade in appearance and surface hardness.

How do you repair cracked plaster in an older Austin home?

Plaster cracks should be cleaned out, stabilized (sometimes with mesh tape or plaster washers for larger failures), filled with compatible plaster material, and texture-matched to the surrounding surface. Using the wrong filler — like lightweight spackle on a hard lime plaster wall — results in a patch that looks and feels different. For older homes, a professional with plaster experience delivers the best result.

Does plaster help with soundproofing?

Yes. Plaster walls are denser and more massive than drywall, which means they block more airborne sound. In practical terms, a plaster wall provides noticeably better sound isolation between rooms than a single layer of standard drywall. For home offices, media rooms, or bedrooms, this is a meaningful benefit.
